lessons.title: Delays & Flip-Flop Timing Parameters
lessons.content:
Every gate and wire takes time to respond. **Propagation delay** $t_{pd}$ is the *maximum* time from a stable input change to a stable output; **contamination delay** $t_{cd}$ is the *minimum* time before the output *starts* to change. So after an input edge the output is guaranteed stable only after $t_{pd}$, and guaranteed unchanged only up to $t_{cd}$. Note $t_{cd} \le t_{pd}$ always.

A flip-flop adds three timing parameters that define a window around its clock edge:

- **Setup time** $t_{su}$ — the input $D$ must be stable for at least $t_{su}$ *before* the active clock edge.
- **Hold time** $t_{h}$ — $D$ must remain stable for at least $t_{h}$ *after* the edge.
- **Clock-to-Q delay** $t_{cq}$ — after the edge, $Q$ is valid after $t_{cq}$ (datasheets often give both $t_{cq,max}$ and a $t_{cq,min}$, akin to $t_{pd}$ vs $t_{cd}$ for combinational logic).

Violate $t_{su}$ or $t_{h}$ and the flop may capture a wrong value or go *metastable* (Lesson 4). As a radar tech you measured rise/fall times on a scope; these are the same physics, now abstracted into datasheet numbers your timing tools consume.

Think of a register-to-register path: the launching flop fires ($t_{cq}$), the signal crosses combinational logic ($t_{pd}$), and must arrive at the capturing flop's $D$ before its $t_{su}$. That chain is the seed of every timing constraint in the rest of this subtopic.

items.front: Define propagation delay $t_{pd}$.
items.back: The **maximum** time from an input change until the output is **guaranteed stable** at its new value. It bounds the worst-case (slowest) path.

items.front: Define contamination delay $t_{cd}$ and its relation to $t_{pd}$.
items.back: The **minimum** time before an output **begins** to change after an input change. Always $t_{cd} \le t_{pd}$. It bounds the fastest path and matters for hold timing.

items.front: What is setup time $t_{su}$?
items.back: The minimum time the data input $D$ must be **stable before** the active clock edge for the flip-flop to capture it reliably.

items.front: What is hold time $t_{h}$?
items.back: The minimum time $D$ must remain **stable after** the active clock edge for reliable capture.

items.front: What is clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq}$?
items.back: The delay from the active clock edge until the flip-flop output $Q$ is valid. It is the time the launching flop adds to a register-to-register path.

items.prompt: Data must be held stable for a window around the clock edge. Which two parameters bound that window?
items.explanation: Setup time $t_{su}$ is measured before the edge and hold time $t_{h}$ after it; together they form the keep-out window for $D$.

items.prompt: Which inequality between a gate's delays is always true?
items.explanation: The output cannot finish settling ($t_{pd}$) before it even starts to change ($t_{cd}$), so $t_{cd} \le t_{pd}$.

items.prompt: On a register-to-register path, which delay does the launching flip-flop contribute before the signal enters the combinational logic?
items.explanation: After the launch edge, $Q$ becomes valid only after $t_{cq}$; that is the launching flop's contribution to the path delay.

items.prompt: A datasheet lists $t_{cq,max}$ and $t_{cq,min}$. Why distinguish them?
choices: They are the same number written twice
$t_{cq,max}$ feeds worst-case (setup) analysis; $t_{cq,min}$ feeds fastest-path (hold) analysis
$t_{cq,min}$ is used only for power estimation
$t_{cq,max}$ applies only to the clock tree
items.explanation: Setup checks use the slowest (max) launch, while hold checks use the fastest (min) launch—analogous to using $t_{pd}$ vs $t_{cd}$ in combinational logic.

lessons.title: The Setup & Hold Inequalities, f_max, and the Critical Path
lessons.content:
Consider two flip-flops separated by combinational logic, both on clock period $T_{clk}$. After a launch edge the data is valid at $D$ of the capturing flop at time $t_{cq}+t_{pd}$, and it must arrive at least $t_{su}$ before the *next* edge. That gives the **setup constraint**:

$$t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su} \le T_{clk}$$

Solving for the period sets the **maximum clock frequency**:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su}}$$

The slowest register-to-register path—the **critical path**—is the one that maximizes $t_{cq}+t_{pd,max}$ and therefore caps $f_{max}$. **Slack** is how much margin a path has: $\text{slack} = T_{clk} - (t_{cq}+t_{pd,max}+t_{su})$; negative slack means a setup failure.

The **hold constraint** is different—it compares fastest data against the *same* edge:

$$t_{cq,min} + t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$$

Notice $T_{clk}$ does **not** appear. Hold is **frequency-independent**: the data launched at an edge must not race through and corrupt the capturing flop's input *before* its hold window closes after that same edge. This is why **slowing the clock can fix setup violations but never hold violations**—a classic rust-knocker. Hold failures are fixed by adding delay (buffers) on short paths or fixing skew, not by lowering frequency.

items.front: State the setup (max-delay) constraint for a register-to-register path.
items.back: $t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su} \le T_{clk}$. The launched data plus worst-case logic plus setup must fit inside one clock period.

items.front: Give the formula for $f_{max}$ from setup timing (zero skew).
items.back: $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su}}$, set by the critical (slowest) path.
items.hint: Reciprocal of the longest path period.

items.front: State the hold (min-delay) constraint and note what is absent.
items.back: $t_{cq,min} + t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$. **No $T_{clk}$ term**—hold is frequency-independent.
items.hint: Fastest data vs. the same edge.

items.front: Define the critical path.
items.back: The register-to-register path with the largest $t_{cq}+t_{pd,max}$; it has the least setup slack and therefore determines $f_{max}$.

items.front: Why can't you fix a hold violation by slowing the clock?
items.back: The hold inequality $t_{cq,min}+t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$ has no $T_{clk}$ term. It's a race against the *same* edge, so a longer period does nothing—you must add path delay or fix skew.

items.prompt: Given $t_{cq}=0.3\,\text{ns}$, $t_{pd,max}=4.2\,\text{ns}$, $t_{su}=0.5\,\text{ns}$ and zero skew, what is $f_{max}$?
A path has $T_{clk}=8\,\text{ns}$, $t_{cq}=0.4$, $t_{pd,max}=6.0$, $t_{su}=0.6\,\text{ns}$. What is the setup slack?
Which variable does NOT appear in the hold-time inequality?
items.explanation: Hold is $t_{cq,min}+t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$; the clock period is absent, which is exactly why hold is frequency-independent.

items.prompt: A design fails hold on a short path between two adjacent flops. What is a valid fix?
choices: Add buffer/delay on the data path or fix the skew
items.explanation: Hold violations need more minimum data delay (buffers) or skew correction; period changes have no effect because $T_{clk}$ is not in the inequality.

lessons.title: Clock Skew, Jitter, and Pipelining
lessons.content:
**Clock skew** is the difference in clock arrival time between the launching and capturing flops, $t_{skew} = t_{clk,capture} - t_{clk,launch}$. Skew shifts both constraints. With skew the setup constraint becomes

$$t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su} \le T_{clk} + t_{skew}$$

Positive skew (clock reaches the capture flop *later*) *relaxes* setup—it effectively lends time. But the same positive skew *tightens* hold:

$$t_{cq,min} + t_{cd} \ge t_{h} + t_{skew}$$

So skew is a double-edged sword; tools budget it carefully, and uncontrolled skew (e.g., from gating a clock) is dangerous.

**Jitter** is cycle-to-cycle variation in the clock edge position—random (phase noise) and deterministic. Unlike static skew, jitter is uncertain, so it eats directly into your timing budget: subtract it from the period for setup. Your radar phase-noise intuition transfers exactly—jitter is phase noise in the time domain.

**Pipelining** raises throughput by inserting registers to chop one long combinational path into shorter stages. If a path of delay $D$ is split into $k$ balanced stages, each stage is $\approx D/k$, so $f_{max}$ rises (ideally by $\approx k\times$, minus the added $t_{cq}+t_{su}$ per stage). The cost is **latency**: results now take $k$ cycles to emerge, and you must add matching pipeline delays to side signals. Pipelining is the standard answer when a critical path won't meet timing—central to FPGA radar DSP, where deep MAC pipelines run at line rate.

items.front: Define clock skew.
items.back: $t_{skew} = t_{clk,capture} - t_{clk,launch}$: the difference in clock arrival time between the capturing and launching flip-flops.

items.front: How does positive skew affect the setup and hold constraints?
items.back: Positive skew **relaxes setup** ($\dots \le T_{clk}+t_{skew}$) but **tightens hold** ($t_{cq,min}+t_{cd} \ge t_{h}+t_{skew}$). It's a trade-off, not free time.

items.front: How does clock jitter differ from skew, and how is it budgeted?
items.back: Skew is a fixed spatial offset; jitter is random/cycle-to-cycle uncertainty in edge timing. Jitter is subtracted from the usable period for setup analysis. It is phase noise viewed in the time domain.

items.front: What does pipelining do to $f_{max}$ and to latency?
items.back: Splitting a path into $k$ balanced stages raises $f_{max}$ by up to $\approx k\times$ (minus per-stage $t_{cq}+t_{su}$), but increases latency to $k$ cycles before the first result emerges.
items.hint: Higher throughput, more cycles of delay.

items.front: When pipelining a datapath, what must you do to parallel/side signals?
items.back: Delay them by matching pipeline registers so they stay aligned with the data they accompany; otherwise control/data become misaligned by the pipeline depth.
items.hint: Keep everything in step, stage for stage.

items.prompt: With positive clock skew $t_{skew}$, the setup constraint becomes:
items.explanation: Positive skew delays the capture edge, effectively adding $t_{skew}$ to the available period and relaxing setup.

items.prompt: A 12 ns combinational path is pipelined into 3 balanced stages of 4 ns each (ignore added $t_{cq}/t_{su}$). The new throughput period is roughly:
items.explanation: Each stage is now the critical path at ~4 ns, so the clock period drops to ~4 ns even though end-to-end latency is still ~12 ns (3 cycles).

items.prompt: Why is gating a clock with an AND-enable considered bad practice for timing?
choices: It introduces uncontrolled skew/glitches on the clock and complicates timing closure
It is the only way to reduce hold violations
items.explanation: Logic in the clock path adds skew and can glitch the clock; use a synchronous clock-enable on the flop instead.

items.prompt: Clock jitter most directly hurts which budget?
choices: It eats into the effective period, tightening the setup budget
It has no effect on synchronous timing
items.explanation: Because the next edge may arrive early, jitter is subtracted from the usable period, shrinking setup margin (just as phase noise degrades a radar's coherence).

lessons.title: Metastability, Synchronizers, CDC & Reset
lessons.content:
When a flip-flop's setup/hold window is violated—inevitable when sampling a signal asynchronous to the clock—its output can hover between logic levels for an unbounded time before resolving randomly. This is **metastability**. It is a physical bistable-circuit phenomenon (a ball balanced on a hill), so it can never be fully eliminated, only made statistically rare.

The figure of merit is **mean time between failures**:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{\,t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk}\, f_{data}\, T_{0}}$$

where $t_{r}$ is the resolution time allowed before the value is used, $\tau$ and $T_0$ are device constants, and $f_{clk}$, $f_{data}$ are the clock and data-event rates. MTBF grows *exponentially* with the resolution time $t_{r}$—the lever we control.

The **two-flop synchronizer** buys one full clock of resolution time: FF1 samples the async input (and may go metastable), and FF2 resamples FF1's output one cycle later, by which time it has almost certainly resolved. That single-bit recipe is the foundation of **clock-domain crossing (CDC)**.

Multi-bit buses can't use parallel two-flop synchronizers—bits would resolve on different cycles, producing illegal intermediate values. Instead: encode counters in **Gray code** (only one bit changes per step, so at most one bit is mid-transition), use a **handshake** (req/ack with the data held stable), or use an **asynchronous FIFO** (the standard for streaming data, combining Gray-coded pointers and dual-clock RAM).

Finally, **reset strategy**: prefer **asynchronous assert, synchronous de-assert**. Assert reset immediately regardless of clock, but release it synchronized to the destination clock so flops don't exit reset near a clock edge and go metastable—the same synchronizer reasoning applied to the reset line itself.

items.front: What is metastability and why is it unavoidable?
items.back: A flip-flop output stuck between valid logic levels for an unbounded time after a setup/hold violation. It's an intrinsic bistable-circuit phenomenon (unstable equilibrium), so it can only be made rare, never eliminated.

items.front: Write the metastability MTBF expression and name the lever you control.
items.back: $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{\,t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk}\,f_{data}\,T_{0}}$. The controllable lever is the resolution time $t_{r}$; MTBF grows **exponentially** with it.

items.front: How does a two-flop synchronizer reduce failure rate?
items.back: FF1 samples the async signal and may go metastable; FF2 resamples FF1's output a full clock later, giving ~one period of resolution time ($t_r$), which raises MTBF exponentially.

items.front: Why can't you synchronize a multi-bit bus with parallel two-flop synchronizers?
items.back: Individual bits may resolve on different cycles, yielding illegal transient combinations. Use Gray-coded counters, a handshake, or an async FIFO instead.

items.front: State the recommended reset strategy and its rationale.
items.back: **Asynchronous assert, synchronous de-assert**: assert reset immediately, but release it synchronized to the destination clock so flops don't exit reset near an edge and go metastable.

items.front: Why is Gray code used for CDC counter (e.g., FIFO pointer) crossings?
items.back: Only one bit changes between consecutive Gray-code values, so at most one bit can be mid-transition when sampled—any captured value is either the old or new count, never an illegal in-between.

items.prompt: In the MTBF expression, increasing which quantity raises MTBF exponentially?
items.explanation: MTBF $\propto e^{t_r/\tau}$; only $t_r$ sits in the exponent. The rate terms in the denominator make MTBF worse as they grow.

items.prompt: What is the role of the FIRST flop in a two-flop synchronizer?
choices: It guarantees a glitch-free output immediately
It samples the async input and absorbs the (possible) metastable event, giving FF2 a full cycle to see a resolved value
items.explanation: FF1 is the one allowed to go metastable; the design relies on it resolving within one clock so FF2 captures a clean level.

items.prompt: You must stream a wide data word from a fast ADC clock domain to a slower processing domain. Best mechanism?
choices: Parallel two-flop synchronizers on every data bit
A single-bit synchronizer on bit 0 only
An asynchronous (dual-clock) FIFO with Gray-coded pointers
Gate the source clock with an enable
items.explanation: Streaming multi-bit data across domains is exactly what an async FIFO solves: data RAM is dual-clock and the read/write pointers cross safely via Gray code.

items.prompt: Why de-assert reset synchronously even when asserting it asynchronously?
choices: So flops don't release from reset too close to a clock edge and risk metastability
Because asynchronous assert is forbidden by all tools
To save the global reset routing resources
items.explanation: Async assert guarantees immediate reset regardless of clock state, while synchronized de-assert ensures all flops leave reset on a clean edge, avoiding a metastable exit.
